figures.caption: Figure 1: Density-stratified coordinate transformation for two cooled

Table 1: DNS flow conditions for the transformation comparisons. Reynolds and Mach numbers follow the notation of the original DNS studies; 𝛿𝑖 , 𝑥𝑠ℎ and 𝐿𝑖𝑛𝑡 are defined in the text. The final column lists the wall-layer variation or channel configuration represented by each DNS set.

Figure 2: Mean-profile collapse for the Zhang-Duan-Choudhari smooth high-speed ZPG flat plates (Zhang et al. 2018). (a) Mean velocity profiles in ordinary wall units (𝑦+, 𝑢+). (b) The same profiles after Trettel-Larsson and GFM transformations, plotted as transformed velocity 𝑈+ versus transformed coordinate 𝑌+; dashed and solid coloured curves denote Trettel-Larsson and GFM, respectively. (c) Profiles after the present transformation, plotted as (𝑌+,𝑈+). Colours in all panels identify Mach number and wall thermal condition. Dashed and dotted black curves denote the viscous law and log law.

Figure 3: Two-dimensional boundary-layer fields for the Mach 2.28 ZPG mean field from the Volpiani-Bernardini-Larsson data (Volpiani et al. 2018, 2023), plotted against streamwise station 𝑥/𝛿𝑖 and wall-normal distance 𝑦+. Here 𝛿𝑖 is the inlet boundary-layer thickness of the DNS, 𝑦+ is the ordinary wall coordinate, and 𝜌+ = 𝜌/𝜌𝑤 . (a) Mean density field 𝜌+. (b) Bounded density correction 𝜒 = 𝐶 (𝑞). (c) Transformed coordinate 𝑌+ with selected coordinate contours. (d) Transformed streamwise velocity 𝑈+. The lower boundary is the wall; the wall condition, wall-distance normal and outer-boundary flux conditions define the transformed coordinate and velocity from which station profiles are sampled.

Figure 4: Boundary-layer transformation comparison. The columns correspond to Trettel-Larsson, GFM and the present transformation. (a-c) Attached high-speed ZPG flat-plate profiles from Zhang et al. (2018). (d-f) Station-resolved Mach 2.28 ZPG boundary-layer profiles from the Volpiani-Bernardini-Larsson data (Volpiani et al. 2018, 2023). Profiles are plotted as transformed velocity versus transformed coordinate. Colours identify the Mach number and wall thermal condition of the Zhang-Duan-Choudhari cases in the upper row; in the lower row grey and pink curves denote the 𝑇𝑤/𝑇𝑟 = 1.00 and 𝑇𝑤/𝑇𝑟 = 1.90 station groups, respectively. Dashed and dotted black curves denote the viscous law and log law.

Table 2: Buffer-layer envelope width Δbuf for the boundary-layer profile sets in figures 4 and 5. Values are the mean difference between the upper and lower envelopes of the plotted profiles over 5 ⩽ 𝑌+ ⩽ 70, after interpolation to a common 𝑌+ grid. Smaller values denote tighter profile collapse within each plotted set.

Figure 5: Cogo high-speed ZPG boundary-layer comparison (Cogo et al. 2023a,b). (a) 𝑀∞ = 2-4 cases. (b) 𝑀∞ ≃ 5.86-6 cases. Each panel compares Trettel-Larsson (green dashed), GFM (blue solid) and the present transformation (orange solid), with thin curves denoting individual Cogo profiles within each Mach-number group. Profiles are plotted as transformed velocity versus transformed coordinate. Dashed and dotted black curves denote the viscous law and log law.

Figure 6: Reference-field transformation through the cooled-wall shock/boundary-layer interaction of Hirai & Kawai (2023). (a) Mean density normalised by the local wall value, 𝜌/𝜌𝑤 (𝑥), over (𝑥 − 𝑥𝑠ℎ)/𝐿𝑖𝑛𝑡 ≃ −3.5 to 4.3, where 𝑥𝑠ℎ is the nominal shock-impingement

Figure 7: Symmetric isothermal channel comparison. (a) Huang-Coleman-Bradshaw channel profiles (Huang et al. 1995). (b) Gerolymos-Vallet channel profiles (Gerolymos & Vallet 2023, 2024). Trettel-Larsson (green dashed), GFM (blue solid) and the present transformation (orange solid) are plotted as transformed velocity versus transformed coordinate. The black dashed and dotted curves denote the viscous law and logarithmic law. The comparison tests the density correction in symmetric isothermal channel flows.
